quiz.title: Unit 9 Quiz 12, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Purity Calculations and Gas Volume Stoichiometry
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Percentage Purity, Gas Volumes at STP, Mass-Mass Stoichiometry, Industrial and Environmental Applications

questions.question:
A 4.50 g sample of commercial baking soda (NaHCO₃) is heated until it completely decomposes. If 0.48 g of water vapor is collected, determine the percentage purity of the baking soda.

Equation: 2NaHCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + H₂O + CO₂
questions.explanation:
Molar mass NaHCO₃ = 84.01 g/mol, H₂O = 18.02 g/mol
From equation: 2 mol NaHCO₃ (168.02 g) → 1 mol H₂O (18.02 g)
Mass ratio NaHCO₃:H₂O = 168.02:18.02 = 9.32:1
Mass pure NaHCO₃ = 0.48 g H₂O × 9.32 = 4.47 g
Percentage purity = (4.47/4.50) × 100% = 99.3%

questions.question:
6.75 g of an impure iron sample is reacted with excess sulfur to produce iron(II) sulfide (FeS). If 9.20 g of FeS is formed, calculate the percentage purity of the iron filings.

Equation: Fe + S → FeS
questions.explanation:
Molar mass FeS = 87.91 g/mol
Moles FeS = 9.20/87.91 = 0.1047 mol
Mole ratio Fe:FeS = 1:1, so moles pure Fe = 0.1047 mol
Molar mass Fe = 55.85 g/mol
Mass pure Fe = 0.1047 × 55.85 = 5.85 g
Percentage purity = (5.85/6.75) × 100% = 86.7%

questions.question:
A 12.50 g sample of contaminated zinc granules reacts with sulfuric acid to release 0.15 moles of hydrogen gas (H₂). Determine the percentage purity of the zinc.

Equation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio Zn:H₂ = 1:1, so moles pure Zn = 0.15 mol
Molar mass Zn = 65.38 g/mol
Mass pure Zn = 0.15 × 65.38 = 9.81 g
Percentage purity = (9.81/12.50) × 100% = 78.5%

questions.question: In the Haber process, nitrogen and hydrogen react to form ammonia: N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) → 2NH₃(g). What volume of H₂ gas is required to react completely with 4.50 × 10⁵ L of N₂ at STP?
questions.explanation:
At STP, volume ratio = mole ratio
Volume H₂ needed = 4.50 × 10⁵ L × 3 = 1.35 × 10⁶ L

questions.question: Natural gas (methane) burns in power plants: CH₄(g) + 2O₂(g) → CO₂(g) + 2H₂O(g). If a plant burns 8.20 × 10⁶ L of methane, what volume of pure oxygen is consumed at STP?
High-heat combustion in car engines produces nitric oxide: N₂(g) + O₂(g) → 2NO(g). If 1.25 × 10⁴ L of NO is produced, what volume of N₂ was reacted at STP?
questions.explanation:
Volume ratio N₂:NO = 1:2
Volume N₂ = 1.25 × 10⁴ L / 2 = 6.25 × 10³ L

questions.question: Nitrogen monoxide is converted to less harmful gases: 2NO(g) + 2CO(g) → N₂(g) + 2CO₂(g). What volume of CO is needed to neutralize 5.00 × 10³ L of NO exhaust at STP?
questions.explanation:
Volume ratio NO:CO = 2:2 = 1:1
Volume CO needed = 5.00 × 10³ L

questions.question:
Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) is a triprotic acid. How many moles of H₃PO₄ are required to completely neutralize 0.600 moles of NaOH?

Equation: H₃PO₄ + 3NaOH → Na₃PO₄ + 3H₂O

How many moles of nitric acid (HNO₃) are needed to neutralize 0.045 moles of Ba(OH)₂?

Equation: 2HNO₃ + Ba(OH)₂ → Ba(NO₃)₂ + 2H₂O

Limewater, Ca(OH)₂, is used to neutralize stomach acid (HCl). How many moles of HCl can be neutralized by 0.0125 moles of Ca(OH)₂?

Equation: Ca(OH)₂ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + 2H₂O

Solid potassium chlorate (KClO₃) is used in some emergency oxygen generators. What volume of O₂ gas at STP is produced from the decomposition of 250 g of 92% pure KClO₃?

Equation: 2KClO₃(s) → 2KCl(s) + 3O₂(g)
questions.explanation:
Mass pure KClO₃ = 250 × 0.92 = 230 g
Molar mass KClO₃ = 122.55 g/mol
Moles KClO₃ = 230/122.55 = 1.877 mol
Mole ratio KClO₃:O₂ = 2:3
Moles O₂ = 1.877 × (3/2) = 2.816 mol
Volume O₂ at STP = 2.816 × 22.4 = 63.1 L

questions.question:
When 'impure' iron(II) sulfide (FeS) reacts with hydrochloric acid, it releases toxic hydrogen sulfide gas. If 45.0 g of 75% pure FeS reacts, what volume of H₂S gas is formed at STP?

Equation: FeS(s) + 2HCl(aq) → FeCl₂(aq) + H₂S(g)
questions.explanation:
Mass pure FeS = 45.0 × 0.75 = 33.75 g
Molar mass FeS = 87.91 g/mol
Moles FeS = 33.75/87.91 = 0.384 mol
Mole ratio FeS:H₂S = 1:1
Moles H₂S = 0.384 mol
Volume H₂S at STP = 0.384 × 22.4 = 8.60 L

questions.question:
Calcium carbide (CaC₂) reacts with water to produce acetylene gas (C₂H₂). If 100 g of a 80% pure CaC₂ sample is used, determine the volume of C₂H₂ gas generated at STP.

Equation: CaC₂(s) + 2H₂O(l) → Ca(OH)₂(aq) + C₂H₂(g)
questions.explanation:
Mass pure CaC₂ = 100 × 0.80 = 80.0 g
Molar mass CaC₂ = 64.10 g/mol
Moles CaC₂ = 80.0/64.10 = 1.248 mol
Mole ratio CaC₂:C₂H₂ = 1:1
Moles C₂H₂ = 1.248 mol
Volume C₂H₂ at STP = 1.248 × 22.4 = 28.0 L

questions.question:
Some dry chemical extinguishers use sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃) which releases CO₂ when heated. What volume of CO₂ at STP is released by the decomposition of 500 g of 95% pure NaHCO₃?

Equation: 2NaHCO₃(s) → Na₂CO₃(s) + H₂O(g) + CO₂(g)
questions.explanation:
Mass pure NaHCO₃ = 500 × 0.95 = 475 g
Molar mass NaHCO₃ = 84.01 g/mol
Moles NaHCO₃ = 475/84.01 = 5.654 mol
Mole ratio NaHCO₃:CO₂ = 2:1
Moles CO₂ = 5.654/2 = 2.827 mol
Volume CO₂ at STP = 2.827 × 22.4 = 63.3 L

questions.question:
An industrial process uses scrap zinc to produce hydrogen gas. If 60.0 g of 88% pure zinc reacts with excess sulfuric acid, what volume of H₂ gas is produced at STP?

Equation: Zn(s) + H₂SO₄(aq) → ZnSO₄(aq) + H₂(g)
questions.explanation:
Mass pure Zn = 60.0 × 0.88 = 52.8 g
Molar mass Zn = 65.38 g/mol
Moles Zn = 52.8/65.38 = 0.808 mol
Mole ratio Zn:H₂ = 1:1
Moles H₂ = 0.808 mol
Volume H₂ at STP = 0.808 × 22.4 = 18.1 L

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You've mastered purity calculations and gas stoichiometry! 🧪📊
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review mass-to-mole conversions and gas volume relationships.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on calculating pure mass from percentage purity first.
scoreMessages.0: Please review stoichiometric calculations, purity concepts, and gas volume at STP.
